metadata.summary: Rails backend patterns for production: Service Objects encapsulating business logic with transactions and result objects; RSpec model/service/request specs with factories; Sidekiq background jobs with retry policies; and proper error handling via ActiveRecord::RecordInvalid.

content.title: Ruby on Rails Backend Development
content.summary: Rails backend patterns for production-grade applications. Service Objects encapsulate business logic (user creation, order processing) with ActiveRecord::Base.transaction for data consistency and ServiceResult for success/failure semantics. RSpec model specs validate constraints, association specs check belongs_to/has_many, scope specs isolate filtering. Service specs exercise the happy path and error cases. Sidekiq background jobs with retry policies (exponential backoff) for async work (email, exports, notifications). Controllers stay thin, delegating to services. Pagination via kaminari.

why.p: Service Objects isolate business logic from HTTP concerns. A Users::CreateService encapsulates "how to create a user" — validations, transactions, audit logging, async notification — separate from "how to respond to POST /users". This separation makes the logic testable without mocking the controller, reusable across API/web/CLI, and maintainable as features grow.

ServiceResult (success/failure) provides explicit contract: the caller knows whether the action succeeded and what data/errors resulted. ActiveRecord::Base.transaction ensures data consistency — if any step fails, all changes roll back. Sidekiq jobs with retry policies decouple critical async work from the request lifecycle.

RSpec specs at the model level test validations and scopes without HTTP overhead. Service specs test the business rule in isolation. Request specs (formerly integration tests) test the full controller-to-model flow, hitting the router and auth layers.

list.item: Any Rails/Ruby backend shipping to production with a database.
Building multi-user SaaS with proper data isolation, audits, and async notifications.
Complex domain logic (order processing, payment reconciliation, report generation).
API backends with strict JSON contracts, validation errors, and fault handling.
Background job pipelines for email, data export, or data transformation.
Simple static sites or content management — Rails is overkill.
Real-time systems requiring sub-100ms latency — consider Go or Rust.
Teams unfamiliar with Rails conventions — the convention overhead requires buy-in.

section.name: service-objects
section.p: Service Objects encapsulate business logic. Initialize with params and dependencies (current_user, logger). Call the service via .call or similar. Return a ServiceResult with success/failure semantics and data/errors. Use ActiveRecord::Base.transaction to ensure atomicity.

rule.statement: Every endpoint that touches the database MUST delegate to a Service Object (Users::CreateService, Orders::ProcessService). The service MUST wrap database operations in ActiveRecord::Base.transaction and return a ServiceResult (success/failure with data or errors).
rule.rationale: Services isolate business logic from HTTP, making it testable without a request and reusable across API/web/CLI. Transactions ensure data consistency. ServiceResult provides an explicit contract so callers don't have to catch exceptions.

rule.statement: A Service MUST raise ActiveRecord::RecordInvalid on validation failures (save!) and catch it to return a ServiceResult.failure. It MUST NOT swallow exceptions; catch specific errors (RecordInvalid, PaymentGatewayError) and re-raise unexpected errors so they bubble to error tracking (Sentry, etc.).
rule.rationale: save! raises when validations fail. Catching RecordInvalid and converting to ServiceResult.failure is idiomatic Rails. Re-raising unexpected errors ensures they bubble to monitoring so on-call engineers know a service is broken.

example.title: Service Object with transaction and result
example.code:
# app/services/users/create_service.rb module Users class CreateService def initialize(params:, current_user: nil) @params = params @current_user = current_user end
def call user = User.new(user_params)
ActiveRecord::Base.transaction do user.save! send_welcome_email(user) create_audit_log(user) end
ServiceResult.success(user) rescue ActiveRecord::RecordInvalid => e ServiceResult.failure(e.record.errors.full_messages) end
private
def user_params @params.slice(:name, :email, :password) end
def send_welcome_email(user) UserMailer.welcome(user).deliver_later end
def create_audit_log(user) AuditLog.create!( action: 'user.created', resource: user, actor: @current_user ) end end end
# app/services/service_result.rb class ServiceResult attr_reader :data, :errors
def initialize(success:, data: nil, errors: []) @success = success @data = data @errors = errors end
def success? @success end
def failure? !@success end
def self.success(data = nil) new(success: true, data: data) end
def self.failure(errors) new(success: false, errors: Array(errors)) end end
example.note: Service encapsulates user creation, email dispatch, and audit logging within a transaction.

example.title: Controller delegating to service
example.code:
# app/controllers/api/v1/users_controller.rb module Api module V1 class UsersController < ApplicationController def create result = Users::CreateService.new( params: user_params, current_user: current_user ).call
if result.success? render json: UserSerializer.new(result.data), status: :created else render json: { errors: result.errors }, status: :unprocessable_entity end end
private
def user_params params.require(:user).permit(:name, :email, :password) end end end end
example.note: Controller is thin: extract params, call service, render result.

section.name: rspec-testing
section.p: Use RSpec for specs. Model specs validate presence/uniqueness/length and association structure. Service specs exercise the business logic (happy path, error cases). Request specs test the full endpoint (auth, routing, JSON shape). Factory Bot creates test fixtures.

rule.statement: Every model MUST have a model spec using shoulda-matchers (validate_presence_of, validate_uniqueness_of, have_one, have_many) to document and test constraints. Scope specs MUST isolate the WHERE clause: create(:user, active: true) and create(:user, active: false), then assert User.active returns only the active one.
rule.rationale: Shoulda matchers read as documentation. Scope specs catch regressions when someone refactors the WHERE logic. Model specs are fast (no HTTP, no full Rails setup) and isolated.

rule.statement: Service specs MUST cover the happy path (valid params → success result, data persisted, side effects dispatched) and error cases (invalid params → failure result with errors, no data persisted).
rule.rationale: Service specs verify the contract: given input, the service returns success/failure. Error cases are as important as happy paths — they document what the service does when things go wrong.

example.title: Model spec with validations and associations
example.note: Model spec documents validations, associations, and scopes.

example.title: Service spec with happy path and error cases
example.note: Service spec covers happy path and validation failure.

section.name: sidekiq-background-jobs
section.p: Use Sidekiq for async work. Define a Job class with sidekiq_options (queue, retry count, dead letter queue). Use sidekiq_retry_in to define custom backoff (exponential, linear per exception type). Catch specific exceptions and re-raise, letting the job framework handle retry.

rule.statement: Every async operation (email, export, data sync) MUST be enqueued as a Sidekiq Job (include Sidekiq::Job, sidekiq_options queue: ..., retry: ...). Jobs MUST catch specific exceptions (PaymentGatewayError) with custom retry logic and re-raise unexpected errors so monitoring systems catch them.
rule.rationale: Sidekiq decouples async work from the request lifecycle. If email dispatch fails, the job retries; the user's account creation is not rolled back. Retry policies ensure transient failures (network blips) are retried; permanent failures (invalid email) fail fast.

rule.statement: Job perform methods MUST handle missing records gracefully (ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound). Jobs MUST log via Sidekiq.logger, not puts, so logs appear in the job queue monitoring system.
rule.rationale: A job may be enqueued for an object that is later deleted. Logging via Sidekiq.logger ensures the message appears in job monitoring dashboards (Sidekiq Pro, ContractuallyObliged, etc.).

example.title: Sidekiq job with retry policies
example.code:
# app/jobs/process_order_job.rb class ProcessOrderJob include Sidekiq::Job
sidekiq_options queue: :default, retry: 3, dead: true
sidekiq_retry_in do |count, exception| case exception when PaymentGatewayError (count + 1) * 60 # Linear backoff for payment issues else (count ** 4) + 15 # Exponential backoff end end
def perform(order_id) order = Order.find(order_id)
return if order.processed?
OrderProcessor.new(order).process!
NotifyCustomerJob.perform_async(order_id) rescue ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound # Order deleted, nothing to do Sidekiq.logger.warn "Order #{order_id} not found" rescue PaymentGatewayError => e # Will retry with custom backoff raise rescue StandardError => e ErrorTracker.capture(e, order_id: order_id) raise end end
# app/jobs/notify_customer_job.rb class NotifyCustomerJob include Sidekiq::Job
sidekiq_options queue: :notifications, retry: 5
def perform(order_id) order = Order.find(order_id) OrderMailer.confirmation(order).deliver_now end end
example.note: ProcessOrderJob uses custom sidekiq_retry_in logic for different exception types. NotifyCustomerJob is simpler.

example.title: Batch processing in Sidekiq
example.code:
# app/jobs/batch_export_job.rb class BatchExportJob include Sidekiq::Job
sidekiq_options queue: :exports, retry: 1
def perform(export_id) export = Export.find(export_id) export.update!(status: :processing)
records = export.query_records
CSV.open(export.file_path, 'wb') do |csv| csv << export.headers records.find_each(batch_size: 1000) do |record| csv << export.row_for(record) end end
export.update!(status: :completed, completed_at: Time.current) ExportMailer.ready(export).deliver_later rescue StandardError => e export.update!(status: :failed, error_message: e.message) raise end end
example.note: Batch export uses find_each(batch_size: 1000) to avoid loading all records into memory.
